coredata.dc:description: The widespread presence of acid red 18 (AR18) in the environment has raised concerns about the potential risks on human health and ecosystems. Herein, upon addition of AR18, nitrogen- doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized with catechol and 3, 3-diaminobenzidine exhibit interactive behavior with AR18. Therein, the emission peaks at 490 nm and 410 nm are quenched by internal filtering effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Notably, the former experiences a rapid decline due to robust FRET efficiencies. On this basis, a N-CDs-based fluorimetric and colorimetric sensor is developed for sensitive and selective detection of AR18 in concentrations ranging from 0.053 to 100 μM with a low detection limit (LOD) of 53 nM. In addition, a smartphone-based dual-recognition device, integrated with composite film that enhances liquid collection in determination zone, is aimed at the sensitive detection of AR18 in food samples. The LODs are determined to be 131 nM and 122 nM respectively under fluorimetric and colorimetric conditions. Concomitantly, the device exhibits satisfying accuracy and precision for practical applications. Therefore, this newly developed dual-recognition device provides reliable means to detect the presence of AR18, demonstrating promising potential for on-site monitoring and intelligent sensing applications.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-recognition sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for visual monitoring of acid red 18 in food

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread presence of acid red 18 (AR18) in the environment has raised concerns about the potential risks on human health and ecosystems. Herein, upon addition of AR18, nitrogen- doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized with catechol and 3, 3-diaminobenzidine exhibit interactive behavior with AR18. Therein, the emission peaks at 490 nm and 410 nm are quenched by internal filtering effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Notably, the former experiences a rapid decline due to robust FRET efficiencies. On this basis, a N-CDs-based fluorimetric and colorimetric sensor is developed for sensitive and selective detection of AR18 in concentrations ranging from 0.053 to 100 μM with a low detection limit (LOD) of 53 nM. In addition, a smartphone-based dual-recognition device, integrated with composite film that enhances liquid collection in determination zone, is aimed at the sensitive detection of AR18 in food samples. The LODs are determined to be 131 nM and 122 nM respectively under fluorimetric and colorimetric conditions. Concomitantly, the device exhibits satisfying accuracy and precision for practical applications. Therefore, this newly developed dual-recognition device provides reliable means to detect the presence of AR18, demonstrating promising potential for on-site monitoring and intelligent sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food dyes, as one of food additives, are widely used during the production of foodstuffs such as soft drinks, tea, sauce, dairy product and lipstick, which can be divided into synthetic dyes and natural dyes according to their sources. When it comes to food additives, synthetic dyes are favored over natural dyes due to better stability, lower price and easier handling. However, the majority of synthetic dyes are primarily composed of azo compounds (-NN-) and contain a chromophore that can break down into aromatic amines, which exhibit toxic carcinogenicity and genotoxicity [1,2]. Acid red 18 (AR18), an azo synthetic red dye known as 1-(4-sulfo-1-naphthylazo)-2-hydroxy-6, 8-naphthalenedisulfonic acid trisodium salt, is widely utilized in food to enhance appearance and taste, thereby attracting consumers [3]. However, with the increased intake of AR18, it not only has potential carcinogenicity but also causes certain diseases such as allergic diseases, hyperactivity in children and asthma [4]. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has announced that the acceptable daily intake of AR18 is inferior to 0.7 mg/kg body weight per day [5]. Consequently, the development of an analytical method for detecting AR18 with accuracy, simplicity and rapidness is necessary.

Currently, the primary methods for AR18 detection have high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [7], capillary electrophoresis [8], immunoassay [9] and electrochemical analysis [10]. However, these methods are expensive, complex to operate, low accuracy, cumbersome pretreatment and time-consuming to analyze, which fail to satisfy the requirements of rapid detection for trace amounts of AR18. In contrast, fluorescence analysis is widely used due to high sensitivity, good selectivity and inherent simplicity. The luminescent characteristics of these fluorescence sensors primarily stem from fluorescence materials, including organic dyes, graphene quantum dots, carbon nanotubes and carbon dots (CDs). Among these, CDs have demonstrated application potential due to their unique low toxicity, versatile applicability, highly tunable fluorescence and photoluminescent properties [11–13]. The majority of the CDs-based fluorescence analyses for AR18 detection still rely on individual sign alterations. For example, Cui et al. developed nitrogen-doped CDs for the selective and sensitive determination of AR18 by the fluorescence peak at 590 nm quenching [14]. However, the instrumental accuracy and stability, background noise and fluorescence probe concentration are interference on these fluorescence analyses [15]. The ratiometric fluorescence analysis uses the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two distinct emission peaks to determine target concentration, thereby exhibiting significant potential for enhancing detection accuracy through mitigation of external and instrumental influences. For examples, Dai et al. fabricated N-CDs to build a fluorescence sensor toward AR18 using the ratiometric change of the two signals with LODs of 16 nM [16]. Guo et al. proposed bimodal excitation N-CDs to evaluate the concentration of AR18, which had an excellent linear relationship [17]. However, practical applications of these single-mode determinations encounter some difficulties due to the susceptibility of fluorescence intensity to various external conditions, including probe concentration, excitation light intensity, detection environment and instrument efficiency [18]. Versus single-mode determination, dual-mode determination, which adopting distinct mode to independently measure the same target, can overcome the inherent limitations of the method and achieve complementary advantages, thereby achieving reduced interference from background signals, enhanced selectivity and more dependable outcomes [19,20]. Meanwhile, in recent years, visual detection based on smartphone has garnered increased focus as potential sample analyzers due to its simple process, portability and ease of operation [21]. Therefore, it has profound importance to construct a smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform for visual monitoring of AR18 in food.

Herein, an optical dual-recognition device based on dual emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is presented for visible detection of AR18. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , N-CDs, synthesized via a solvothermal reaction in ethanol using catechol and 3, 3-diaminobenzidine (DAB) as precursors, exhibit dual emission peaks at 410 nm and 490 nm, respectively. With the concentration of AR18 increasing, N-CDs draw closer to AR18 through interactions for instance π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions, thereby facilitating the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). Due to variations in FRET efficiencies, the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm significantly quenches and the fluorescence intensity at 410 nm gently decreases. As such, a fluorimetric and colorimetric sensor is developed for AR18 detection with a low detection limit (LOD) as low as 53 nM from 0.053 to 100 μM. In addition, a novel smartphone-based dual recognition detection device is innovated for the determination of AR18 through colorimetric alterations under two different conditions, which has the LODs of 122 nM and 131 nM, respectively. Besides this, the device demonstrates an average recovery within the acceptable range of 95.0–112.0% in five real samples, with relative standard deviation (RSD) under ultraviolet (UV) light below 2.43%. Additionally, the average recovery under day light falls within the acceptable range of 93.0–113.0%, accompanied by RSD lower than 2.48%. All in all, our work not only establishes a highly sensitive dual-mode device capable of visual AR18 detection under different lights, but also accelerates the development of the field in dual-recognition sensor.

DAB (99.00%), catechol (99.00%), AR18 (99.99%), ethanol (≥99.99%), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 99.99%), NaCl (99.50%), KCl (99.50%), ZnCl2 (99.99%), MgSO4 (99.99%), FeCl3 (99.99%), AlCl3 (99.99%), FeCl2 (99.99%), CaCl2 (99.90%), CeCl3 (99.99%), PbCl2 (99.99%), AgCl (99.99%), HgCl2 (98.00%), BaCl2(99.99%), CdCl2 (99.99%) and other chemicals were bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All solvents and reagents were used as received and deionized water with a resistance of 18.2 MΩ was used throughout all experiments from the Millipore system (Millipore, Billerica, MA). The sodas, wine, tea and yoghurt were obtained from the nearby market surrounding Ningbo University (Ningbo, China) and Yongjiang river samples directly extracted from Yongjiang river (Ningbo, China).

The N-CDs were synthesized using DAB as nitrogen source and catechol as oxygen source according to the solvothermal method with some modifications [22,23]. Briefly, catechol (0.101 g, 0.94 mmol) and DAB (0.103 g, 0.47 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol. Then the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave liner and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Following the cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min and purified through a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the resulting small molecules. Finally, the purified solution was dried by a freeze dryer to obtain N-CDs powder.

The morphology properties and size distribution of the N-CDs were characterized with atomic force microscope (AFM) (Oxford Instruments, Cypher S) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Model JEM-2100 F, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Ltd.). For TEM analysis, TEM was operated at 200 kV and fitted with Gathan CCD camera and N-CDs were dispersed in acetone and drop cast on the 300-mesh carbon coated Cu grid. Samples for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were prepared by drop-casting N-CDs powder onto the Si wafers. For XPS survey spectra, the beam diameter was 500 μm and the pass energy (PE) was 150 eV. Nicolet FT-IR-iS5 was used to identify the N-CDs surface group functionalities using an FT-IR spectrum. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed by X-Ray Diffractometer (Cu Kα, 2θ range 10–50°). Raman spectra were obtained using a HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800 Evolution Raman spectrometer equipped with an argon (20 mW) laser as the excitation source, operating at an exciting wavelength of 540 nm. Zeta potentials of samples were performed in ethanol medium using Malvern instrument. The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra (including photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra) were recorded by UV-5800 spectrophotometer and PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.), respectively. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were determined at room temperature using Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer. The Xiaomi 11 was used to capture the digital images. The HPLC analysis experiment was conducted utilizing an Agilent HPLC system from the 1200 series from Santa Clara (CA, USA), equipped with a photodiode array detector (PAD). A Eurospher-100C8 (5 μm, 250 × 4.6 mm) column and Eurochrom 2000 software were used throughout.

The QY of the N-CDs was measured following the method in previous report. Specially, rhodamine 6G was chosen as the reference. Various N-CDs solutions with different concentrations were prepared to ensure that all associated optical absorbance values within the range of 0–0.1. The QY of N-CDs was then calculated using the following equation [24].
(1)
Q Y =

where QY means the quantum yield, I denotes the integrated fluorescence emission intensity, K stands the gradient of integrated emission intensity against absorbance and η refers the refractive index (1.36 for ethanol). The subscripts ‘‘ST” and ‘‘X” are used to indicate the reference and target sample, respectively.

Typically, a fix amount 150.0 μL of purified N-CDs solution and varying concentrations of AR18 solution (0.053–600 μM) were separately added into the cuvette and diluted with phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0) to 3 mL at 25 °C. The resulting solution was shaken to ensure complete mixing before capturing the fluorescence emission spectra. Fluorescence emission spectra were tested in the excitation wavelength of 356 nm after shaking well and incubating continuously for 5 min. The linearity of the constructed sensor to the target analyte was an indispensable parameter that can be used to validate analytical method. The I490/I410 (I490 and I410 were the fluorescence intensities in the test solutions, respectively) was linearly dependent on the increasing concentrations of AR18 in the range of 0–600 μM. To ensure the accuracy of the experimental results, parallel measurements were conducted three times.

To investigate the selectivity of N-CDs towards AR18, various interference substances (Na+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Folic acid, Sucrose, Glutathione, Glucose, Arginine, Threonine, L-methionine, DL-methionine, Glycine, Histidine, Alanine, Leucine, Fructose, Basic red 5, Indigo, Amaranth, Metanil yellow, Auramine O, Sudan I, Quinoline yellow) were separately added into the solution and their corresponding fluorescence intensity was observed. Additionally, the anti-interference ability was conducted by quantifying the fluorescence intensities of solutions prepared through the addition of 1 mL of various influence substances to a 1 mL solution with N-CDs, accompanied by 1 mL of AR18. The reactions were performed in triplicate to minimize potential errors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polyvinyl alcohol and N-CDs composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The elementary film was prepared by mixing N-CDs into non-luminous polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). Specifically, a mixture of 0.1 g PVA and 0.01 g N-CDs powder in 30 mL water was stirred at 80 °C for 30 min. To achieve proper dispersion, the mixture was subjected to ultrasound treatment for 10 min. Afterward, 5 mL of the N-CDs and PVA mixture was transferred to a Petri dish and left to air dry at room temperature for 24 h. In addition, the N-CDs/PVA films were divided into small heart films with identical diameter. Subsequently, a novel composite film was fabricated through the obtained N-CDs/PVA heart films incorporated into the castor oil-based waterborne polyurethane (CWPU) films that was modified using aminopropyltriethoxysilane (KH550) [25]. Thus, the new composite film consisted of the hydrophobic domain (modified CWPU film) and the determination zone (N-CDs/PVA film), providing convenience for determination.

Under day and UV lights irradiation, the RGB values of the composite film were analyzed by the software (Color Picker) from the images captured by the smartphone. To further ensure the precision and sensitivity of the smartphone imaging colorimetry method, we chose the concentration of AR18 as the abscissa, the R/G and G/B values as the ordinate to establish two distinct linear correlation, respectively. To be brief, the standard curve of R/G ratio and AR18 concentration was obtained for visual quantitative analysis of AR18 under the day light. Meanwhile, the G and B were employed to calculate the RGB ratio under UV light. According to the linear regression equations of the standard curve, the AR18 concentration was determined in the samples.

To confirm the practicality of the purified N-CDs, sodas, wine, tea, yoghurt and Yongjiang river were chosen as the research objects. To reduce the interference of impurities, the samples were subjected to the following treatment process before analysis. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of AR18 (1 μM, 50 μM and 100 μM) with a facile pre-treatment. After sonication for 30 min, all the samples were filtered using 0.22 μm membrane and then the resulting filtrate was dissolved in water. Subsequently, the liquid samples after treatment were spiked with the varying levels of AR18 and thoroughly mixed for subsequent analysis. Afterward, the sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Eventually, the detection of AR18 used the fluorescent probe in real samples according to the aforementioned procedure by using a fluorescence spectrometer.

The determination of AR18 content in food samples was also determined by HPLC. The analysis was conducted using an Agilent HPLC system (Santa Clara, CA, USA) from the 1200 series, equipped with a photodiode array detector (PAD). The chromatographic separation was carried out by using a C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm id stainless steel) from Waters Corporation (Milford, MA, USA). The mobile phase was consisted of ammonium acetate and methanol and the mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 10.0 mmol/L ammonium acetate (90:10, v/v). The flow rate was set as 1.0 mL/min and the injection volume was 30.0 μL. The UV detection was monitored at 508 nm. Three replicate assays were performed for each measurement.

The N-CDs are prepared using catechol and DAB as precursors at 200 °C for 8 h in ethanol. The synthesis process of N-CDs is shown in Fig. S1. Initially, due to the highly functional –NH2 groups of DAB, oxidation reactions occur, resulting in the production of oxidized DAB (oxDAB) [26]. Meanwhile, the two active phenolic hydroxyl groups of catechol undergo oxidation, leading to the creation of o-benzoquinone [27]. Subsequently, Schiff reaction takes place between the CO of o-benzoquinone and the –NH2 group of DAB/oxDAB products. After that, the subsequent processes follow through dehydration, condensation and polymerization, eventually forming various polymer fragments [28]. These polymers undergo further carbonization and aromatization to form aromatic clusters under high temperature of 200 °C and ultimately transforming into N-CDs.

The size distribution and morphology of obtained N-CDs are characterized through TEM (Fig. 1 (a)). It is evident that all the materials exhibit dot-shaped and finely disperse without noticeable aggregation. The HR-TEM image (inset in Fig. 1(a)) displays a typical well-resolved crystal lattice with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, indicating a high crystallinity quality. The size of the N-CDs is revealed to disperse in a range of 2.0–6.0 nm with a mean diameter of 4.2 nm (Fig. 1(b)). As shown in Fig. 1(c), the XRD pattern displays a broad diffraction peak at 24.3° corresponding to a crystal lattice with 0.23 nm. The AFM image of N-CDs reveals that the topographic height of N-CDs is predominantly distributed within 1.0–4.6 nm, indicating the successful synthesis and primarily consisting of a few layers of graphene-like sheets (Fig. 1(d)). As illustrated in Fig.S2, the Raman spectrum of N-CDs exhibits two distinct peaks at 1369 cm−1 (disorder D bond) and 1590 cm−1 (graphite G bond). The ratio of D to G bands is 0.38, indicating a significant level of graphitization of N-CDs [29]. The FT-IR and XPS are used to study the terminal elements and functional groups of N-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1(e), the N-CDs show the broad absorption bands from 3350 to 3585 cm−1, indicating a large amount of hydroxyl (-OH) or amino (-NH) groups existing on the surface [30]. The vibration of C–H bonds shows characteristic peak between 2839 cm−1 and 2969 cm−1. The stretching vibration peaks of C–O, O–H, C–N, CN and CO are located at 1006 cm−1, 1253 cm−1, 1387 cm−1, 1454 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1, respectively [31]. The full XPS spectrum of N-CDs presented in Fig. 1(f) shows three peaks at 284.48 eV, 399.68 eV and 530.78 eV, illustrating that the N-CDs consist of C, N and O. Meanwhile, the atomic concentrations of C, N and O in N-CDs (Fig. 1(f)) are calculated to be 74.99%, 8.17% and 16.84%, respectively. To be more specific, the deconvolution of C 1s high-resolution spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV and 288.7 eV, which are attributed to CC/C–C, C–N/C–O and CO/CN (Fig. 1(g)), respectively [32]. From the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1(h)), four divided peaks at 397.48 eV, 398.35 eV, 399.29 eV and 400.07 eV are obtained, representing pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, amino N and graphitic N [33]. Among the O 1s high-resolution spectrum of N-CDs, CO (530.6 eV) and C–O (532.1 eV) can be observed in Fig. 1(i) [34]. These results are consistent with the analysis using FT-IR spectroscopic, indicating that the N-CDs surface has numerous nitrogen-containing and oxygenated functional groups.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy are used to illustrate the optical properties of N-CDs at room temperature. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 2 (a) shows the absorption at 224 nm may be attributed to π-π* transition of CC while the absorption at 276 nm may relate to n-π* transition of CO [35]. Besides, the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2(a)) illustrates that under excitation wavelength of 356 nm, the N-CDs exhibit dual emissions peaks at 410 nm and 490 nm. The inset shows that N-CDs display faint yellow color with day light and cyan fluorescence under UV light, respectively. In addition, the N-CDs display a wavelength-dependent pattern at a variety of excitation wavelengths (300–410 nm) (Fig. 2(b)), implying that the fluorescence primarily originates from specific surface chemical groups [36]. Meanwhile, the 3D mapping of PL excitation and emission wavelength in Fig. 2(c) reveals the two distinct emission peaks at 410 nm and 490 nm under the optimal excitation of 356 nm. Furthermore, the QY of N-CDs is computed to be 26.1%. Moreover, PL stability of N-CDs is studied at various experimental conditions to evaluate their feasibility and availability. As depicted in Fig. 2(d-e), the relative PL intensity of N-CDs experiences negligible decrease when exposed to UV light for 24 h and irradiation of visible light for 24 days, demonstrating the potent radiation resistance of N-CDs. Besides, the relative PL intensity of N-CDs in the presence of varying concentrations of NaCl solution is investigated in Fig. 2(f). Remarkably, even with an increased NaCl medium concentration up to 2 M, the fluorescence emission of N-CDs exhibits a sustained high intensity. These results collectively suggest the prospective applications of the produced N-CDs in various domains, particularly in sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral response of N-CDs toward AR18
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best performance of N-CDs for the sensing of AR18, several important parameters such as the N-CDs concentration, pH and reaction time are optimized. To be specific, as illustrated in Fig. S3(a), the fluorescence intensity ratio (I490/I410) of N-CDs exhibits an initial increase followed by a subsequent decrease with the increasing concentration of N-CDs. Subsequently, while the concentration of N-CDs is 40 ng/μL, the fluorescence quenching efficiency to AR18 is the highest, resulting the selection of the N-CDs concentration (40 ng/μL) for subsequent experimental investigations. As shown in Fig. S3(b), in the absence of AR18, the value of I490/I410 ascends gradually as the pH value fluctuated from 3 to 6 and reaches a maximum at pH = 7, then decreases from pH 8 to 13. Meanwhile, in the presence of AR18, the optimal quenching phenomenon is observed at pH = 7. Furthermore, to explore the feasibility of the developed sensor with rapid detection, suitable incubation time is examined. The reaction time between the AR18 and N-CDs is observed in the range of 0–3 min (Fig. S3(c)), where the I490/I410 quenches rapidly within 2 min and tends to stabilize. To obtain a stable result, we choose 2 min as the optimal response time. There, 40 ng/μL, pH = 7 and 2 min of incubation time are applied in the subsequent sensing experiments.

Under the optimal conditions, the quantitative detection of AR18 is realized according to the change of fluorescence intensity at two emission wavelengths. As illustrated in Fig. 3 (a), the PL intensity of N-CDs at 490 nm progressively diminishes as the concentration of AR18 increasing, exhibiting a significant higher intensity reductio compared to that at 410 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I490/I410 reduces with the AR18 concentration ranging from 0 to 600 μM (Fig. 3(b)). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 3(c), a remarkable linear correlation (R2 = 0.9959) is observed at the AR18 concentration range of 0.053–100 μM and the corresponding linear equation can be expressed as I490/I410 = −0.00198C[AR18]+1.11836 (“C[AR18]” is the concentration of AR18). Additionally, the LOD is determined to be 53 nM using formula 3σ/k (where σ represents the standard deviation of blank solution and k represents the slope of the calibration curve). Of particular note, after adding different concentrations of AR18, the solution shows faint yellow to red change under day light, while the color of the solution changes from clear cyan to faint blue within UV light, which can be observed visually by unaided eye (Fig. S4). As shown in Table S1, our proposed probe for detecting AR18 present lower LODs which indicate that this sensor is more sensitive. In addition, this probe also has wider detection range to extend its further application under larger range of AR18 concentrations condition. Moreover, unlike other sensors based on single signal change, our probe not only has fluorescence intensity change with good linear relationship, but also possesses visual color-changing effects. Overall, this method constructed from dual emission N-CDs has a comparable or even better performance over other methods.

To evaluate the selectivity of this probe for AR18 detection, the influences of common metal ions (Na+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+), small molecular interferers (Folic acid, Sucrose, Glutathione, Glucose, Fructose), amino acids (Arginine, Threonine, l-methionine, dl-methionine, Glycine, Histidine, Alanine, Leucine) and food colorants (Basic red 5, Indigo, Amaranth, Metanil yellow, Auramine O, Sudan I, Quinoline yellow) are investigated. As shown in Fig. S5(a), it is obvious that only AR18 obviously suppress the PL intensities of N-CDs while the other possible interfering substances have nearly negligible impacts on the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs, indicating the exceptional selectivity of N-CDs for AR18 sensing. As depicted in Fig. S5(b), the relative PL intensities of the N-CDs@AR18 sensing system remain largely stable after mixed with the above potential interfering substances, suggesting that the N-CDs@AR18 sensing system has high anti-interference capability. Additionally, a significant color variation under the identical conditions is easily observed by the naked eye in Fig. S6. There above results resoundingly verify the high sensitivity and selectivity of N-CDs for nitrite detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for fluorescence sensing toward AR18
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, we conduct a series of studies to delve into the potential mechanism of the presented sensor for AR18. First of all, the UV–vis absorption spectra of AR18 (red line), N-CDs (blue line), the mixtures of N-CDs@AR18 (black dotted line) and the sum of blue line and red line (green line) are exhibited in Fig. 3(d). Compared with green line, a slight red shift can be observed on the absorption peak of N-CDs@AR18 at 510 nm, which may be ascribed to the binding interaction between N-CDs and AR18 [37]. Therein, the binding constant (K
BH ), reflecting the interaction strength between N-CDs and AR18, is calculated to further confirm the binding action between N-CDs and AR18 through the Benesi-Hildebrand Equation (2) [38].
(2)
1

where I
s and C represent the fluorescence intensity with a saturated analyte concentration and the concentration of analyte. Meanwhile, I
0 and I are fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of analyte. As shown in Fig. S7, the value of K
BH is as high as 1.27 × 104 mol−1, revealing an intense attraction between N-CDs and AR18 [39]. Furtherly, FT-IR, XPS and zeta potential are analyzed to elucidate binding interactions between N-CDs and AR18. Interestingly, the stretching vibrations of C–O (917 cm−1) and CO (1625 cm−1) exhibit a blue shift, indicating that a reduction in the percentage of carboxylic group and the formation of hydrogen bonding (Fig. S8) [40]. Besides, compared to the original N-CDs baseline, the binding energy of pyrrole N peak noticeably shifts toward lower values, which coincides with a decrease of content (Fig. S9). According to the literature, the π-π electron donor-accepter (EDA) interaction between AR18 molecule and pyrrole N occurs, which can influence pyrrole N content on N-CDs [41]. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. S10, the zeta potential of 14.54 mV for N-CDs suggests that their surfaces are positively charged while the zeta potential of AR18 is −11.34 mV. It is found that the zeta potential of N-CDs experiences a reduction to 4.23 mV with the addition of AR18, which confirms the occurrence of electrostatic interaction owing to the opposite polarity [42]. Therefore, it is speculated that the distance between N-CDs and AR18 can be shortened by hydrogen bonding, π-π EDA interaction and electrostatic interaction, which ensure the feasibility of IFE and FRET. Besides, it is evident that the absorption peak of AR18 significantly overlaps with the excitation and emission peak of N-CDs (Fig. 3(e)), which corresponds to the characteristics of IFE and FRET quenching phenomena [43].

To better confirm the existence of IFE, further validation is performed by the Parker Equation (3) and Equation (4) as follow, which are based on the cuvette geometry and the absorption characteristics of N-CDs and AR18 [44–46].

where I
obsd refers to the measured fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 490 nm under the excitation of 356 nm; I

obsd and E
cor ) are calculated through I
obsd and I
cor values. And the calculated results are listed in Table S2 and Table S3 below. As the increase of AR18 concentration, the suppressed efficiencies of observed and corrected (i.e., after removing IFE) fluorescence exhibit an upward trend (Fig. S11). The suppressed efficiencies of observed fluorescence at 490 nm and 410 nm reach 75.6% and 67.1%, respectively, while the suppressed efficiencies of corrected fluorescence are as high as 41.9% and 34.2%. These results suggest that IFE is occurred between AR18 and N-CDs, meanwhile FRET may be coexistent in the quenching system [47].

Furthermore, to better confirm the existence of FRET, as shown in Fig. 3(c), it can be seen that the introduction of AR18 causes evident change of the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs. Specifically, the average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs@AR18 and N-CDs at 410 nm are 2.81 ns and 2.97 ns, respectively. Similarly, the average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs with and without AR18 at 490 nm are 3.29 ns and 4.28 ns (Table S4). The overt variations of average PL lifetimes and spectral overlap suggest that the predominated fluorescence quenching is due to FRET [48]. As we known, the efficiency of FRET is influenced by the overlap area between the absorption spectrum of the acceptor molecule and the emission spectrum of the donor molecule when their separation remains constant [49]. The efficiency of FRET is calculated with the average fluorescence lifetimes by the following Equation (5) [50].
(5)
E = 1 −
τ
D A
τ D
where E denotes the efficiency of FRET while τ
D and τ
DA denote the value of fluorescence lifetime of the donor before and after FRET, respectively. The results demonstrate that E
410 (5.69%) is lower than E
490 (23.13%) (Table S4), illustrating that more FRET efficiency exists between the fluorescence peaks at 490 nm and AR18, leading to the PL intensity of 490 nm more significantly quenched [51]. Thus, we deduce that the fluorescence reaction of N-CDs to AR18 is a main factor of FRET and a subordinated factor of IFE, meanwhile, the distinct efficiencies of FRET between the two peaks lead to varying degrees of quenching observed in each peak. The schematic diagram of the related sensing mechanism is presented in Fig. 3(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-recognition analysis device for detecting AR18
ce:section.ce:para: Smartphone imaging can be utilized as a robust tool in the orientation of analytical chemistry, significantly enhancing the development of naked-eye detection methods. Here, a smartphone sensor device based on N-CDs/PVA composite film, which takes advantage of the high-resolution camera and portability of the film, is designed to analyze color parameters during real-time detection. As shown in Fig. S12, a smartphone-based analysis device consists of a portable dark acrylic box, a mobile phone, a detachable push-pull drawer and two light sources, which shine day light and UV light. Fig. 4 (a) illustrates the complete sensing procedure of detecting AR18 based on the N-CDs/PVA composite film by the smartphone imaging colorimetry. After the interaction of the N-CDs/PVA composite film with different AR18 concentrations, the color information (RGB value) of the film shows a distinct variation under two lights irradiation [52]. In brief, as shown in Fig. 4(b), the color images of composite film under two different lights are converted into digital values and the corresponding color scale cards are created using the software (Color picker), meanwhile each experiment is repeated 3 times. To further improve the anti-interference and accuracy of the imaging colorimetry, the ratio of R/G and AR18 concentration are chosen as the vertical and horizontal coordinate parameters respectively to set up an internal relationship (R/G = 0.00697CAR18+1.254), with a LOD of 122 nM in Fig. 4(c). Meanwhile, as exhibited in Fig. 4(d), the linear relationship between the concentration of AR18 and G/B of N-CDs is rapidly established under UV light. Precisely, the linear relationship of assaying AR18 by the fluorescence (0.131 μM–100 μM) is fitted as G/B = −0.00363CAR18+0.904 with R2 of 0.9951 and LOD is calculated to be 131 nM. Furthermore, the CIE chromaticity diagrams confirm the feasibility of detecting AR18 with naked eyes (Fig. S13). These findings fully demonstrate that the smartphone-based analysis device provides instrumental tool for the accurate and quantitative AR18 determination in real samples.

To validate the effectiveness and dependability of the developed device, sodas, wine, tea, yoghurt and Yongjiang river are collected and analyzed. It is evident that the significant color changes of the composite films occur under day light and UV light after adding the sample (Fig. 4(e)). As presented in Table S5, under the day light, the recovery of AR18 in actual sample is 93.0–113.0% with the RSD of lower than 2.48%. Simultaneously, the recovery and RSD of AR18 based on the UV light are within the range of 95.0–112.0% with the RSD of 1.11–2.43%, respectively. Notably, the results of day light and UV light detections basically exhibit consistent results. Furthermore, the same samples are further examined utilizing a typical HPLC technique as the standard method to evaluate the precision of the detection results. As shown in Table S5, the recovery of AR18 in actual sample is 96.0–107.0% and the RSD is 1.21–2.16%. Additionally, the t-test shows no significant differences in these methods at a 95% confidence level. Then, these results indicate that dual-recognition device has the potential for rapid, low-cost and visual detection of AR18 in food samples. Besides, the practical application capability of the system is further assessed in Fig. S14. To accelerate the detection of AR18, the established linear relationships are transformed into the automated calculator equations. Next, the values of RGB are directly input and then clicking the button of “Calculate” button, yielding instant output of the final result (Video S1). The measured concentrations of AR18 (99.2 μM and 101.2 μM) closely align with the standard value of 100 μM. Furthermore, the results reveal that the various concentration of AR18 detected by this device is generally consistent with the known concentrations of AR18 (Fig. S15). Overall, the whole process from photographing to acquiring the results is completed in a significantly short time, while yielding outcomes closely align with the actual results. Consequently, these findings further validate the high feasibility of our cost-effective sensing system for detecting AR18 in everyday applications.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.112098

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article.
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

To sum up, N-CDs derived from DAB and catechol through solvothermal way display dual-emission centers located at 410 nm and 490 nm under the excitation at 356 nm. Due to different efficiencies of FRET between the emission peaks of N-CDs and AR18, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on N-CDs can achieve a wide range of AR18 detection range from 0.053 to 100 μM with a LOD of 53 nM. Meanwhile, the RGB value of the N-CDs@AR18 changes significantly and differentially under day and UV lights, which paves the way for structuring a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for the visual detection of AR18. Besides, the smartphone-assisted device is integrated with the N-CDs/PVA composite film comprising the hydrophobic domain (modified CWPU film) and determination zone (N-CDs/PVA film) to realize the on-site AR18 residue analysis. Most interestingly, the device has determination of AR18 through colorimetric alterations under two different conditions, which has the LOD of 122 nM and 131 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the AR18 in food samples is rapidly detected with the satisfied recovery rates of 93.0–113.0% under day light and 95.0–112.0% under UV light condition. To our knowledge, this is the first report about the AR18 determination using a dual-recognition platform crafted form the specialized composite film and smartphone-assisted device. Thus, this device introduces a fresh idea to optical dual-mode sensor for AR18 visual detection in food.
